svg.title: Why Early Helicopters Failed — Interactive STEM Simulator
svg.desc: An interactive educational SVG with four tabs showing power-to-weight, materials, control, and torque/stability challenges in early helicopter design.
svg.text: Explore the engineering traps: power-to-weight, heavy materials, unstable controls, and torque reaction.

g.text: Touch sliders, tabs, and invention buttons
Can your early helicopter lift off?
Early inventors often had engines that were powerful enough to spin parts, but too weak and heavy for useful vertical flight.
Goal: high power, low engine mass, safe total weight.
Simplified lift score uses power loading:
Increase power or reduce engine weight.
Historical anchor: practical helicopters had to wait for engines with much better power-to-weight ratios, then still needed good control systems.
A rotorcraft needs materials that are light, strong, fatigue-resistant, and stiff enough to hold blade shape.
Try lighter + stronger to approach modern composite behavior.
Fails: too heavy or too weak
Modern rotor blades use light alloys, fiberglass, carbon fiber, and sandwich/honeycomb structures for high strength-to-weight and fatigue resistance.
Lift-off is not enough — can the pilot control it?
Early machines could hop, shake, drift, or spin. Reliable control required several linked inventions.
Add cyclic + collective + swashplate + damping to turn a jump into controlled flight.
Torque reaction: the body wants to spin the other way
A single main rotor twists the fuselage in the opposite direction unless an anti-torque system balances it.
Try a tail rotor or coaxial counter-rotating rotors to cancel torque.
Bonus: fins and stabilizers help steady forward flight, but they do not by themselves cancel hover torque.

Simplified for Grade 5 STEM: values are scaled teaching indices, not certified aircraft design limits. The history/science notes are based on NASA, FAA, Sikorsky Archives, ASME, and aerospace education sources.
